title: 2010_flow_field_and_combustion_characteristics_of_premixed_g
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 2]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**

    *   The page mentions burner pressure drops as "Δp between 1% and 3%, where the percentage refers to the absolute pressure upstream of the burner."  This can be re-written as: Δp = (x/100) * p_upstream, where x is between 1 and 3.

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

    *   There are no explicit graphs or charts on the provided page.

3.  **Tables:**

    *   The text refers to "Table 1" for the details of the OH* chemiluminescence/PLIF measuring technique. Since the table is not included in this page, no additional information is possible.

4.  **Diagrams/Schematics:**

    *   **Figure 1:** This is a cut-away drawing of the combustor. The diagram shows the following major components:
        *   A swirl burner that mixes preheated air with natural gas. The airflow is aided by a radial swirler.
        *   Fuel inlet
        *   Preheated seeding air inlet. The preheated air is seeded with  TiO2 particles for PIV (Particle Image Velocimetry) measurements.
        *   Metal sidewall
        *   Quartz glass windows. They each have two quartz plates with a cooling airflow in between.
        *   Exhaust gas transition duct
        *   Exhaust outlet

    *   **Figure 2:** This is an experimental setup diagram for OH PLIF and OH* chemiluminescence imaging. The setup includes:
        *   A laser system and a dye cell. The system is set up to generate the appropriate wavelength for exciting the OH radical. It includes components such as convex lenses, concave cylindrical lenses, and beam splitters to adjust the laser beam.
        *   A matrix burner and flame for laser adjustment, and a photomultiplier tube to detect OH LIF signal from the flame.
        *   The combustor is used for the actual measurements.
        *   Two ICCD (Intensified Charge Coupled Device) cameras: one to image OH PLIF and OH* chemiluminescence. The other camera records the laser intensity profile after passing through a dye cell, enabling PLIF image correction

    *   The text also refers to a **Figure 3**:
        *   This schematic shows the PIV setup utilizing a flashlamp-pumped Nd:YAG laser. Since the figure is not included int his page, no further information is possible.

title: 2010_flow_field_and_combustion_characteristics_of_premixed_g
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 3]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**

    *   f1 = -12.7 mm
    *   f2 = 200 mm
    *   f = 1000 mm

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

    *   Figure 3: This is a schematic diagram of the PIV (Particle Image Velocimetry) experimental setup. It shows the relative positions and optical paths of the following components:
        *   PIV Laser: The light source for the PIV measurements.
        *   Beam expansion (cylindrical lenses) and Convex lens: These optical elements shape and focus the laser beam into a thin sheet.
        *   Aperture: Used to control the size and shape of the laser beam.
        *   Combustor: The combustion chamber where the PIV measurements are taken.
        *   Filter (532 nm): Selects the light from the PIV laser
        *   PIV camera (ICCD camera): Captures the images of the particles illuminated by the laser sheet.
        *   Filter (300-325 nm): Used on a ICCD camera to select the relevant wavelengths for OH\* chemiluminescence.
        *   Video camera: Used for control purposes only.
        *   ICCD camera: observes the OH\* chemiluminescence.

3.  **Tables:**

    *   Table 1: Technical details of the PLIF (Planar Laser-Induced Fluorescence) and Chemiluminescence diagnostics:
        *   *OH* chemiluminescence measurement

            | Parameter            | Value               |
            | :------------------- | :------------------ |
            | Exposure time        | 40 μm            |
            | Frame rate           | 3 Hz                |
            | Effective image size | 512x446 pixels          |
            | Bandpass filter      | 300-325 nm |

        *   OH PLIF measurement

            | Parameter            | Value               |
            | :------------------- | :------------------ |
            | Exposure time        | 400 ns           |
            | Laser pulse duration           | 8 ns                |
            | Laser pulse energy  | 4.6-5.5 mJ                |
            | Laser wavelength      | 283.55 nm          |
            | Effective image size | 359x446 pixels          |
            | Bandpass filter      | 300-325 nm |

        *   ICCD camera specifications (both cameras)

            | Parameter            | Value               |
            | :------------------- | :------------------ |
            | Dynamic range        | 16 bits             |
            | CCD chip size           | 512x512 pixels                |
            | Objective (flame)  | UV Halle, 100 mm, f/2                |
            | Objective (dye cell)     | UV Nikkor, 105 mm, f/4.5          |

    *   Table 2: Technical details of the PIV setup:

        | Parameter             | Value            |
        | :-------------------- | :--------------- |
        | Laser pulse duration  | 5 ns             |
        | Laser pulse energy    | 120 mJ           |
        | Laser sheet width      | 1 mm            |
        | Double-pulse delay    | 10-15 μs        |
        | PIV repetition rate   | 5 Hz             |
        | Seeding material      | TiO2 (1 μm)      |
        | PIV camera objective | Nikkor,

title: 2010_flow_field_and_combustion_characteristics_of_premixed_g
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 4]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**
    *   No explicit mathematical equations are presented in the text, but several parameters are mentioned. These include:
        *   p = 3 bars (or 4 bars) refers to the pressure in the combustion chamber.
        *   Δp = 1% is likely the pressure drop across some component, possible the fuel injector, as a percentage of the chamber pressure.
        *   Φ = 0.59 (or 0.55) refers to the equivalence ratio, which represents the ratio of fuel to oxidizer relative to the stoichiometric ratio.

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

    *   **Figure 4:** Streamline plots of instantaneous Planar Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV) measurements.
        *   **Axes:** The axes are spatial dimensions within the combustor. There are no labeled axes/values.
        *   **Trends:** Shows the instantaneous flow field in the burner. Velocity magnitude is color-coded; the direction is given by the streamlines. Three successive instantaneous measurements (a, b, c) show temporal variation in the flow field. Notable features include the inflow region near the burner nozzle, vortex structures in the shear layers, and direction of the flow being toward the burner mouth. Arrows indicate local flow direction. The text states the repetition rate was 5 Hz, implying turbulent fluctuations.
        *   **Key Data Points:** The burner nozzle size is indicated by two marks on the left side. Pressure p = 3 bars, pressure drop delta p = 1%, and equivalence ratio phi = 0.59.
        *   **Conclusions:** The flow field is highly unsteady and contains vortices. The backflow rate of reaction products in the burner is varying significantly due to the combustor fluid mechanics.
    *   **Figure 5:** Instantaneous distribution of OH\* chemiluminescence.
        *   **Axes:** Spatial dimensions of the combustor
        *   **Trends:** False-color image showing the intensity of OH\* chemiluminescence. The color scale ranges from zero intensity (black) to maximum (white), representing OH radical concentration. Shows a zone of primary reaction with significant inhomogeneities.
        *   **Key Data Points:** The burner exit is marked by a white ellipse. The location of the maximum signal is at a distance of 5-8 cm downstream.
        *   **Conclusions**: High concentrations of OH radicals (and therefore heat release) are observed in a region a few centimeters downstream of the burner exit. This indicates the primary reaction zone location. Combustion occurs with events of local ignition and extinction.

3.  **Tables:** There are no tables.

4.  **Diagrams or Schematics:**
    *   There are no clear diagrams or schematics. The images in figures 4 and 5 are visualization of measured data.

title: 2010_flow_field_and_combustion_characteristics_of_premixed_g
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 5]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**

    *   No explicit algebraic equations containing the "=" symbol are present. There are descriptions of relative parameters denoted using "delta" notation:
        *   Ap = 1.1%, p=1.5 bars, Phi=0.53
        *   Ap = 3%, p=4 bars, Phi=0.50
        *   Ap = 1%, p=4 bars, Phi=0.56

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

    *   **Figure 6:** Single-shot OH PLIF (Planar Laser-Induced Fluorescence) mappings.
        *   **Axis:** Intensity of OH radicals expressed are in % scaled from 0 to Max.  Two images are presented:
            *   **(a)**  Conditions: Pressure (p) = 1.5 bars, delta p = 1.1%, Equivalence ratio (Phi) = 0.53.  This shows the spatial distribution of OH radical concentration in a single laser shot.  Regions of high OH concentration (represented as yellow/light gray in B/W) indicate flame fronts and zones of active combustion.  Black areas indicate regions of relatively low temperature and low OH concentration.
            *   **(b)**  Conditions: Pressure (p) = 4 bars, delta p = 3%, Equivalence ratio (Phi) = 0.50.  The increased pressure appears to lead to a more fragmented flame structure with more numerous, smaller zones of high OH concentration. The regions of shear flow are more extended from the burner.
    *   **Figure 7:** Absolute gradient of OH PLIF intensity.
        *   **Axis:** Absolute gradient of OH-PLIF intensity is shown using a scaled color bar from -50 % to Max. The figure shows the spatial difference in OH radical concentrations across two laser sheets at different points in time to illustrate the instantaneous shape of the flame front collage at p=4 bars.
    *   **Figure 8:** Mean flow field (sum of correlation).
        *   The figure displays the average flow field by a set of scaled vectors with background that contours correspond to mean OH density at p=4 bars, delta p=1%, and equivalence ratio of (phi) = 0.56. The vectors show the direction and relative magnitude of the flow. Backflow is observed inside the recirculation zone.

3.  **Tables:**
    *   No tables are present.

4.  **Diagrams or Schematics:**
    *   No diagrams were present.

title: 2010_flow_field_and_combustion_characteristics_of_premixed_g
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 6]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:** There are no explicit equations displayed on the page. The values such as p=4 bars and phi=0.50 are parameters rather than equations.

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**
    *   **Figure 9:** Shows a diagram representing the averaged flow direction near the burner. There are no axes because it's a flow map. Arrows depict the direction of the planar flow. It visually demonstrates the inflow and recirculation zones (IRZ). The text states that the inflow region can be clearly distinguished from the recirculation zones.
    *   **Figure 10:** A plot of the radial distribution of the axial component of the flow velocity (v_ax) over radial position (r), averaged for a range 7-16 mm downstream of the burner exit. The y-axis is axial flow speed in arbitrary units. The x-axis is radial position (r), no units are provided. Two curves are shown, one for Δp = 3% and another for Δp = 1%. Both curves show a local minimum at r close to 0 (centerline), then increase to maxima at approximately symmetric radial locations, then decreasing again as radial position increases beyond these maxima. The curve for Δp=3% shows slightly greater central minima flow velocity and greater peak flow velocities than dp=1%. This plot provides quantitative information about the flow field near the burner exit.
    *   **Figure 11:** Mapping of the standard deviation (RMS) of the flow velocity at p=3 bars, Δp=1%, and Φ=0.59. The grayscale indicates the magnitude of the RMS fluctuation. Superimposed white arrows indicate the mean flow direction. There are no explicit axes. The scale indicates grayscale range from 0 to maximum RMS. This shows the turbulent regions. The text explains the strongest variations are within shear layers, and turbulent zone is detected downstream.
    *   **Figure 12:** The figure represents a line-of-sight mapping of the mean OH* chemiluminescence distribution for different pressure drops (Δp) over the burner at p=4 bars and ≈0.50. The burner nozzle is marked by a white ellipse. The Chemiluminescence intensity is shown using a grayscale, ranging from 0 to 50% to maximum. Three different pressure drops are shown: (a) Δp=1%, (b) Δp=2%, and (c) Δp=3%.  As Δp increases, the region of OH* chemiluminescence intensity expands axially and radially, and appears to locate closer to combustor sidewalls.

3.  **Tables:** There are no tables on this page.

4.  **Diagrams or Schematics:**
    *   **Figure 9** shows a simplified schematic of a swirling flow field inside a combustor. The planar flow direction from -90° to 90° is depicted with inflow and recirculation zones. This provides a qualitative overview of the flow field close to the burner.

title: 2010_flow_field_and_combustion_characteristics_of_premixed_g
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 7]
1.  **Equations:**
    *   There are no explicit equations on this page.

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

    *   **Figure 13: Radial OH* Distribution.**
        *   **Axes:**
            *   x-axis: radial position r/R (dimensionless, normalized by burner nozzle radius R).
            *   y-axis: OH* density [a.u.] (arbitrary units).
        *   **Curves:**
            *   Four curves representing different pressures: p = 1.5 bar, p = 3 bar, p = 4 bar, and p = 6 bar. The 1.5 bar, 4 bar and 6 bar are all solid lines, the 3 bar curve is a dashed line.
        *   **Trends & Key Data Points:**
            *   The OH* density profiles show a generally increasing OH* density near *r/R* = 0.2, possibly indicating the location of the flame zone. Shaded region at the beginning suggests this region suffers from bad data post-processing.
            *   As pressure increases, the peak OH* density appears to increase and shift slightly towards the center of the combustor (lower *r/R*).
            *   The width of the OH* distribution appears to narrow with increasing pressure.
        *   **Conclusions:**
            *   The radial distribution of OH* chemiluminescence is influenced by the pressure. Higher pressures lead to higher peak OH* densities possibly indicating a strong reaction from the flame. Higher pressures may lead to narrower flamer profile.

3.  **Tables:**
    *   There are no tables on this page.

4.  **Diagrams or Schematics:**

    *   **Inset image in Figure 13:** Abel-inverted OH* chemiluminescence. A false-color image depicting the spatial distribution of Abel-inverted OH* chemiluminescence. The image is semi circle and represents cross section of the burner. The regions marked on edge are the analysed in the curves.
    *   **Description:** This inset image presents a visual representation of the spatial distribution, showing relative intensity of OH* radicals within the flame.
        *   Conclusion the image is used to highlight how the radial profiles correspond to the flame structure.
